meta.build_type: One-day hackathon MVP, solo non-developer builder (QE background)
meta.build_tool: Claude Code (multi-agent orchestration: solution-architect, rapid-builder, qe-guardian)

business_problem.statement: After every candidate interview, HR is left with unstructured, hand-typed notes and no consistent way to turn them into a hiring decision. Manually re-reading notes to extract a judgment is slow and produces inconsistent conclusions across reviewers.
business_problem.persona: HR recruiter / hiring manager managing a candidate pipeline end-to-end
business_problem.market_gap: ATS tools (Greenhouse/Lever) offer only a free-text scorecard field with no summarization. Interview-intelligence tools (BrightHire/Metaview/HireVue) do AI summarization but require recorded/transcribed calls, integration setup, and enterprise pricing. No competitor takes notes HR already typed by hand and returns a structured judgment with zero setup.
business_problem.value_proposition: Turn unstructured interview notes into a consistent, structured hiring judgment (strengths/concerns/rating/recommendation) in seconds, with zero integration or setup cost.

user_roles.description: Single user role for this MVP. Adds candidates, pastes notes, triggers summarization, reviews and saves results, reopens candidate records later.
user_roles.permissions: Full access to all app functions; no role differentiation implemented (single-user, no auth by explicit hackathon scope decision).

functional_requirements: Add a new candidate by name
Select an existing candidate from a list and load their prior notes/summary
Paste free-text interview notes into a text area
Trigger AI summarization of the notes on demand (button click)
Receive a structured result: strengths (list), concerns (list), overall_rating (1-5), recommendation (short string)
Persist the notes + summary against the candidate's record
Reload a candidate's notes + summary automatically when reselected
Run in a usable fallback mode (mocked summary) when no live AI API key is configured, clearly labeled as such
non_functional_requirements: Single command startup, no manual setup steps beyond documented API key + dependency install
No authentication, no multi-user support, no persistence layer beyond a single local file (explicit scope cut for a one-day disposable build)
Never show a raw stack trace or unhandled exception to an end user/judge
Must run from a fully offline/no-external-account fallback path (mocked mode) in case live API access is unavailable
Deployable to a publicly reachable URL without requiring a paid account or approval-gated signup
Portable across at least 3 different hosting targets without app code changes (proves architecture is not tied to one platform)

implemented: AI-powered structured summarization (Claude API, forced tool-use)
Persistent SQLite storage keyed by candidate name
Save button to commit a summary to storage
Automatic reload of a candidate's notes/summary on reselect
Mocked demo-mode fallback when no API key is present, clearly labeled with a warning banner
Clean error handling for: missing API key, blank input, malformed/partial AI response

Multi-target deployment: containerized (Docker), orchestrated (Kubernetes/minikube), publicly tunneled (Cloudflare Tunnel), plus documented fallback paths (Streamlit Community Cloud, Render.com, remote K8s + Docker Hub/ACR)
planned_not_built: Shortlist ranking across multiple candidates with comparative reasoning
Natural-language Q&A search across all candidates' stored history
Live API integration (blocked in this build by company policy, not a technical gap)

workflows.name: Core summarization workflow
steps: Picks an existing candidate OR adds a new one by name
Pastes free-text interview notes into the text area
App checks for a configured API key
If key present: calls Claude API with a forced tool-call and a fixed JSON schema
If key absent: falls back to a labeled mocked example result
Result (strengths/concerns/rating/recommendation) is rendered readably on screen
Result + original notes are upserted into SQLite keyed by candidate name
On any future visit, selecting that candidate reloads the saved notes and summary automatically

business_rules: A candidate record is uniquely identified by name (UNIQUE constraint) — no duplicate-name candidates.
Notes are draft-only until 'Save' is clicked — switching candidates before saving discards unsaved notes (accepted UX tradeoff, not a bug).
The Save button only renders once a summary result exists in the current session — saving before summarizing is structurally impossible through the UI.
A missing/invalid API key must never surface as a raw error — always resolve to either a mocked result (demo mode) or a single clean, human-readable message.
Any AI response missing a required field (strengths/concerns/overall_rating/recommendation) is treated as invalid and surfaces a clean error rather than being partially trusted.

elements: Sidebar: candidate picker (selectbox: existing names + '+ Add new candidate'), conditional new-candidate name text input
Notes textarea (pre-filled if a saved candidate is selected)
Result panel (conditional): Strengths list, Concerns list, Overall Rating, Recommendation, optional '⚠️ DEMO MODE' warning banner if running on a mocked result
'Save' button (conditional: only rendered once a result exists)

database_schema.engine: SQLite (single file, `candidates.db`, colocated with the app)

tables.name: candidates
tables.upsert_strategy: INSERT ... ON CONFLICT(name) DO UPDATE SET ... (idempotent save-by-name)

apis_integrations.name: Anthropic Claude API
apis_integrations.call_pattern: Single messages.create() call with tool_choice forcing exactly one tool call (record_interview_summary), input_schema defines strengths/concerns/overall_rating/recommendation, required + additionalProperties:false enforced
apis_integrations.auth: API key via ANTHROPIC_API_KEY env var, falling back to Streamlit secrets.toml if present (checked for file existence before touching st.secrets to avoid a Streamlit-internal error leak)
cost_controls: single-shot stateless call, no chat history sent
compact structured schema (string arrays, not prose)

validations: Blank/whitespace-only notes blocked before an API call is attempted (st.warning, no call made)
Blank candidate name handled gracefully (subheader shows a placeholder, save requires a name)
AI response validated for required fields before being trusted/rendered; missing fields raise a caught, user-readable error
API key presence checked via a dedicated get_api_key() function before every summarization attempt

authentication.reasoning: Explicit hackathon scope cut — single-user internal tool, no auth/multi-user support added per the project's own standing rule to avoid production-concern scope creep in a one-day disposable build.
authentication.note_for_future: If productionized, would need per-recruiter accounts and row-level access control on the candidates table.

reusable_components.description: Using tool_choice to force exactly one named tool call with a strict input_schema is a reusable pattern for any 'extract structured judgment from unstructured text' feature, not just this HR use case.

reusable_components.pattern: Env-var-first, secrets-file-fallback API key resolution with existence-check guard
reusable_components.description: get_api_key() pattern: check env var first, then check a secrets file actually exists on disk before touching the secrets API, to avoid framework-internal error leakage. Reusable in any Streamlit app reading a secret.

When an external dependency (API key) is unavailable, fall back to a clearly-labeled mocked response rather than only showing an error — keeps a demo/dev flow usable. The real-vs-mock branch is a single boolean check, and switching to live is a zero-code-change config addition.
INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE keyed on a natural unique field (name) — simple persistence pattern for single-file-storage MVPs without needing a migration framework.

Streamlit session_state used to track 'loaded_for' candidate key so switching selection resets/reloads state predictably, with an explicit accepted tradeoff (unsaved drafts are discarded) documented rather than silently left ambiguous.

The same Dockerfile + app code deployed successfully (or was prepared for) 4 different targets: Streamlit Community Cloud, Render.com (via render.yaml blueprint), remote Kubernetes + Docker Hub/ACR, and local Kubernetes (minikube) + Cloudflare Tunnel — proving the app itself carries zero platform lock-in.

reusable_components.pattern: No-signup public tunnel for constrained environments
reusable_components.description: Cloudflare Tunnel's anonymous quick-tunnel mode (`cloudflared tunnel --url`) gives a public HTTPS URL with zero account/signup — a reusable fallback whenever normal cloud hosting is blocked by org policy or account friction.

best_practices_applied: Adversarial QE pass before considering the build done — deliberately tried blank/malformed input, repeated runs, and the missing-key path; caught and fixed a real bug (duplicate/confusing error message) before the user ever saw it
Never show raw stack traces to an end user — every failure path resolves to a clean, human-readable message
Explicit, written known-issues log with severity and demo-time workaround per issue, rather than silently hoping nothing breaks live
Version pins relaxed (>= instead of ==) after a real breakage (pillow wheel unavailable for a newer Python) rather than fighting the platform's runtime
Secrets never committed to source control or embedded in code; read from env var/secret store only
When a credential was accidentally pasted into chat, it was treated as compromised immediately and the user was told to rotate it, regardless of whether it was actually misused
Deployment target decisions documented with the real reasoning (why X was chosen, why Y was abandoned) in DEPLOY.md, not just the final answer

what_worked_well: Splitting work across specialized sub-agents (architecture, build sprints, QE) kept each pass focused and let the QE pass catch a real bug the build agent didn't
Building a mocked-mode fallback turned a hard external blocker (no obtainable API key) into a fully demoable app instead of a dead end
Diagnosing deployment failures from actual error/log output (Python 3.14 pillow wheel failure, DNS resolution difference between resolvers) rather than guessing led to fast, correct fixes
Keeping the same app code stable across every deployment pivot (Streamlit Cloud → Render → remote K8s → local K8s) proved the architecture wasn't the problem, the hosting environment was

Verifying claims instead of trusting them (re-testing the public tunnel via a DNS bypass before telling the user it worked) caught that the office network's DNS, not the deployment, was the actual issue
what_was_missing_initially: No upfront intake of the user's actual environment constraints (company API-key policy, network/OAuth restrictions, existing cloud/cluster access) before choosing a deployment target — this caused 3 reactive pivots that could likely have been reduced with an earlier constraints check
No initial check on whether the target Python/runtime version of the chosen hosting platform matched the pinned dependency versions
No established secure channel for credential handoff at the start of the conversation — the user pasted a secret into chat twice despite being told each time not to

errors_encountered_and_root_causes.error: GitHub push failed with 'could not read Username' / 'Device not configured'
errors_encountered_and_root_causes.root_cause: Non-interactive shell has no way to prompt for git credentials; git also no longer accepts account passwords over HTTPS, only tokens.
errors_encountered_and_root_causes.fix: User ran git push in their own interactive terminal with a classic PAT (repo scope).

errors_encountered_and_root_causes.error: GitHub API repo-creation call returned 403 'Resource not accessible by personal access token'
errors_encountered_and_root_causes.root_cause: Fine-grained PAT lacked account-level repo-creation permission.
errors_encountered_and_root_causes.fix: Abandoned automated repo creation; user created the repo manually via the GitHub web UI.

errors_encountered_and_root_causes.error: Streamlit Cloud build failed with 'Error installing requirements' → pillow build failure
errors_encountered_and_root_causes.root_cause: Exact version pin (streamlit==1.39.0) pulled an old pillow (10.4.0) with no prebuilt wheel for the platform's Python 3.14 runtime.
errors_encountered_and_root_causes.fix: Relaxed pins to >= instead of == so pip/uv could resolve a compatible pillow version.

errors_encountered_and_root_causes.error: GitHub OAuth sign-in for Streamlit Cloud returned a 504 Gateway Timeout
errors_encountered_and_root_causes.root_cause: Corporate network/proxy interfering with the OAuth callback handshake.
errors_encountered_and_root_causes.fix: Retried from outside the office network.

errors_encountered_and_root_causes.error: Anthropic Console org creation blocked with 'Parent Organization is blocking new organization creation for domain'
errors_encountered_and_root_causes.root_cause: Company-wide admin policy on the user's work email domain.
errors_encountered_and_root_causes.fix: No resolution obtained (IT declined); routed around via a mocked demo-mode fallback in the app instead of a real key.

errors_encountered_and_root_causes.error: ACR/Docker registry push attempted with a company credential pasted directly in chat
errors_encountered_and_root_causes.root_cause: User pasted a live secret into the conversation despite being told the correct (file-based) hand-off method beforehand.
errors_encountered_and_root_causes.fix: Credential was flagged as compromised immediately, not reused beyond the immediate step, and rotation was recommended; approach was abandoned in favor of a different registry/target anyway.

errors_encountered_and_root_causes.error: kubectl/docker not found when invoked via the automated tool session
errors_encountered_and_root_causes.root_cause: The automation shell's PATH did not include Homebrew's install location (/opt/homebrew/bin) even though tools were correctly installed via the user's interactive terminal.
errors_encountered_and_root_causes.fix: Invoked binaries by full path / explicitly prepended /opt/homebrew/bin to PATH per command.

errors_encountered_and_root_causes.error: sudo-gated install steps (Homebrew, admin prompts) could not run from the automated tool session
errors_encountered_and_root_causes.root_cause: No interactive TTY available for password entry in that session.
errors_encountered_and_root_causes.fix: User ran those specific steps themselves in their own interactive terminal.

errors_encountered_and_root_causes.error: Public tunnel URL unreachable for the user specifically
errors_encountered_and_root_causes.root_cause: Office network's DNS resolver failed to resolve the tunnel's hostname (trycloudflare.com), while public resolvers (8.8.8.8/1.1.1.1) resolved it fine — a network-level filtering pattern consistent with earlier blocks.
errors_encountered_and_root_causes.fix: Verified the app/tunnel worked correctly by bypassing DNS in test requests; user worked around it locally via a hosts-file entry or a different network.

prompt_improvement_opportunities: Initial problem description was informal/ambiguous and required a full clarifying round-trip — a structured intake prompt (business goal, core AI feature, input format, users) up front reduces this to one pass.
Single-word confirmations ('yes', 'proceed') following a multi-option question are ambiguous and caused repeated re-asks — prompts/responses should reference which specific option is being confirmed.

The deployment target was chosen and pivoted reactively 4 times — a single upfront question about company network/API/account constraints would have surfaced the eventual blockers (no external API key policy, OAuth/DNS network filtering, no confirmed remote cluster access) before any hosting attempt began.

The first presentation deck was built following a generic 'keep slides sparse' best practice by default, which did not match this user's actual need (a content-dense technical+business deck) — prompts building a presentation/deliverable should explicitly ask what level of content density and audience (spoken pitch vs. leave-behind technical doc) is wanted before applying a default style.

Credentials were twice typed directly into the conversation despite instructions to use a file-based hand-off — the instruction should be given as a hard requirement with a concrete file path *before* any credential is ever needed, not just as an in-the-moment warning.

optimized_prompt.purpose: A single upfront prompt that reduces the iteration seen in this session to (ideally) one build pass, for use as a template by an AI application development framework building similar 'unstructured input → structured AI judgment → persisted record' internal tools.
optimized_prompt.prompt_text:
Build a one-day MVP hackathon app: [DOMAIN, e.g. 'an HR tool that summarizes interview notes']. Core AI feature: [describe the single AI-powered transformation, e.g. 'HR pastes free-text interview notes for a candidate; an AI agent extracts a structured summary (strengths, concerns, overall rating, recommendation) and persists it against that candidate's record']. Input format: [e.g. 'free-text, typed directly into the app, no file upload']. Primary user: [e.g. 'single internal user, no auth/multi-user needed']. 

Before building, confirm these constraints so the deployment target is chosen correctly the first time: (1) Is there an existing, usable API key/credential for the AI provider, or is there an organizational policy that blocks provisioning a new one? (2) Is the company network known to block or filter any of: OAuth sign-in flows, specific external domains/DNS, or public tunnel services? (3) Is there an existing remote server/cluster/registry already accessible (with confirmed working credentials in hand), or should the target be a local-machine deployment with a public tunnel? (4) Should the final presentation/deck be a sparse spoken-pitch style or a dense technical+business leave-behind document?

Build order: (a) working core happy-path end-to-end with one hardcoded record before any persistence or secondary UI, (b) add persistence + full CRUD-lite UI, (c) add a labeled mocked-response fallback mode that activates automatically whenever the real API key/credential is unavailable, so the app is always demoable regardless of question (1)'s answer, (d) run an adversarial QE pass (blank/malformed input, repeated runs, missing-credential path) and fix anything that would show a raw error to an end user, (e) deploy to the target chosen in question (3), verifying end-to-end reachability (not just 'it deployed') before considering it done, (f) produce a demo script (exact click sequence + timing) and a presentation matching the density chosen in question (4).

Hard rules: never accept a pasted credential/secret in conversation — always request it be saved to a file path outside the source repo, on the very first ask, before it's ever needed. Never leave a build undemoable due to a missing external credential — always have a mocked fallback. Confirm which option is being actioned when a user gives a one-word response to a multi-option question, rather than assuming.

structured_variables.domain: string — the business domain (e.g. HR, sales, support)
structured_variables.core_ai_transformation: string — the single unstructured-input-to-structured-output feature being built
structured_variables.network_constraints: array — known blocked domains/flows (OAuth, DNS, tunnels), or 'none_known'
